coredata.dc:title: Efficiency improvement of luminescent solar concentrators using upconversion nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Saeidi, Sahar
Rezaei, Behzad
Irannejad, Neda
Ensafi, Ali Asghar
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) incorporated with a large number of luminophores are used as an important trend which increases the efficiency of solar cells by concentrating solar energy onto photovoltaics (PVs). However, the main loss in LSC-PV devises results from the incomplete utilization of the solar spectrum. Extending the spectral absorption from UV–Vis into near-infrared (NIR) by means of upconversion particles, reduces the transmission loss of sub-band-gap photons with improved energy alignment. In this study, a new nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQD) possessing upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL), single color emission in UV–Vis–NIR region has been introduced. Large stocks shift of newly synthesized GQDs that is more than 100 nm reduces reabsorption losses significantly. Photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield is measured to be 65%. Under solar radiation, the optical features of N-GQDs as absorbers/emitters in polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) is studied. Our results demonstrate that GQDs-LSC attached to a perovskite solar cell could enhance efficiency. The GQDs-LSCs reaches the power conversion efficiency (PCE) more than 4.3 times of PMMA-PV devise. Although the use of N-GQDs hybrid in LSCs for its upconversion feature is at an initial research step, the results prove that it provides a promising way for low cost and nonhazardous solar energy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescent solar concentrator
Graphene quantum dots
Upconversion luminescent
Excitation wavelength-independent
Green synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Temperature dependence and excitation dependence of upconversion emission

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry and Research Council of Isfahan University of Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry and Research Council of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) for supporting this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-GQDs, the inset shows the DLS result and size distribution of dots; (b) X-ray diffraction data for the as-synthesized N-GQDs.
FT-IR spectrum of obtained Glucose GQDs and N-GQDs.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-GQD in aqueous solution.

(a) Typical FL spectra of obtained N-GQDs, and the inset shows two photos under 365 nm UV light in the absence (left) and presence (right) of N-GQDs, (b) UCPL spectra obtained from N-GQDs under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) PLE spectrum of N-GQDs in emission wavelengths of 450 and 480 nm in the absence and presence of a cut-off filter of 400 nm, respectively; (b) the intensity of fluorescent and upconversion emission at excitation wavelengths of 320 and 720 nm.
The plot of PL integrated intensity, excited by the selected light of 320 nm for normal PL (circles) and 720 nm for UCPL (squares) as a function of (a) temperature and (b) excitation power density.

(a) IPCE curve of perovskite solar cell and UCPL spectra of N-GQDs in water and GQDs-LSC, (b) J–V characteristics of perovskite solar cells attached to PMMA and GQDs-LSC for light harvest under AM 1.5 G illuminated.
Comparison between the performance of GQDs-LSC-PV and PMMA-PV attached to perovskite solar cell.

head.ce:title: Efficiency improvement of luminescent solar concentrators using upconversion nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156-83111, IR, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156-83111, IR, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) incorporated with a large number of luminophores are used as an important trend which increases the efficiency of solar cells by concentrating solar energy onto photovoltaics (PVs). However, the main loss in LSC-PV devises results from the incomplete utilization of the solar spectrum. Extending the spectral absorption from UV–Vis into near-infrared (NIR) by means of upconversion particles, reduces the transmission loss of sub-band-gap photons with improved energy alignment. In this study, a new nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQD) possessing upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL), single color emission in UV–Vis–NIR region has been introduced. Large stocks shift of newly synthesized GQDs that is more than 100 nm reduces reabsorption losses significantly. Photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield is measured to be 65%. Under solar radiation, the optical features of N-GQDs as absorbers/emitters in polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) is studied. Our results demonstrate that GQDs-LSC attached to a perovskite solar cell could enhance efficiency. The GQDs-LSCs reaches the power conversion efficiency (PCE) more than 4.3 times of PMMA-PV devise. Although the use of N-GQDs hybrid in LSCs for its upconversion feature is at an initial research step, the results prove that it provides a promising way for low cost and nonhazardous solar energy.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) introduced as devices with two functionalities, the first one is the absorption of incident photons of sunlight and emission with different wavelengths by luminophores, and the second one is waveguiding of the photons onto a photovoltaic (PV) cell [1,2]. LSCs are planned to decrease the price of generated electricity as a result of concentrating photons and using only a small side area of expensive PVs [3,4]. In these devices, emitted photons are transported over macroscopic distances and converted to electricity by the PVs [5]. In this case, LSCs have become one of the research subjects with wide applications in smart windows, fiber optics, active color filters and side accessories of electronic devices [6]. Along with waveguiding, different ways reduce the efficiency of LSCs like scattering from the surface, reabsorption by the luminophores or emission into the escape cone defined by Snell's law [7]. The waveguides were made of glass or plastic as a matrix and luminophores include organic dyes, metal oxides or semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) which were doped into the matrix [3,6]. QDs with the advantages of tunable absorption and emission wavelength, high photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) and photostability are considered to be used widely in LSCs [8,9]. GQDs, as a subdivision of luminescent QDs, having low toxicity and cost in comparison with traditional semiconductor QDs, are more interesting for LSCs [10]. GQDs are zero-dimension allotropes of graphene in which carbon atoms are placed in a hexagonal lattice and the structure is similar to an unrolled zero-dimensional finite carbon nanotube [11]. Exclusive optical and electrical properties of GQDs including high luminescence, excellent photostability, biocompatibility, robust chemical interest, two-photon induced fluorescence, and abundance of raw materials in nature make them suitable for applications including photovoltaic cells, optical detection probes, energy storage devices, photocatalysts, biosciences, light-emitting diode materials, and sensors [10,12–14]. Hydrothermal synthesis has been employed for carbonizing organic precursors, makes it possible to have narrow particle size distribution, control the morphology and lattice dimensions of products [15]. Modification of the component and photoluminescence properties of GQDs by adding secondary reagent containing nitrogen, sulfur, magnesium, chlorine, and fluorine is possible. Doping of heteroatoms like nitrogen, within the matrix of graphene could tune the bandgap and increase the radiation transmissions, which results in higher photoluminescence QY and makes them an excellent choice for LSCs [12].

In this paper, a new N-doped GQD employing glucose as an inexpensive and non-toxic carbon source was synthesized. Tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) with five amino groups was chosen as a rich source of nitrogen. Easy fabrication, excitation-independent PL, cost-effective and environmentally friendly are some of the great characteristics of new N-GODs that make it suitable for industrial applications. Moreover, N-GQDs show upconversion emission which authorizes the usage of NIR region with a 52% share of the solar spectrum [16]. The N-GQDs photoluminescence QY is much higher in comparison with similar ones and approves that it is a great luminophore to fabricate LSCs [17,18]. To evaluate the properties of newly synthesized N-GQDs, it was incorporated into polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) using a molding method by controlling the temperature. The functionality of prepared GQDs-LSC was compared with a pure PMMA, attached to a perovskite solar cell, and the results show that the PCE of GQDs-LSC-PV has increased.

D-(+)-glucose (≥99.5%) and Tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA, ≥99.5%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Methyl methacrylate (MMA, 99%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.5–38.0 wt %), and Rhodamine B as reference color (Rh.B) were purchased from Merck and 2, 2′-azobis (2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN, 99%) was purchased from Acros Organics. DI-water used for synthesis.

Glucose (1.00 g), TEPA (400 μL), and HCl (200 μL) dissolved in 15 ml water and keep stirring to achieve a clear solution. A 25 ml Teflon lined autoclave was used to synthesize the N-GQDs and heated for 6 h and 180 °C. The as-prepared brown solution was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 30 min. The same GQD was prepared in the absence of TEPA to compare the characteristics with N-GQDs.

The Philips CM30 300 transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at 300 kV and dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern ZEN 3600) were employed to obtain the shape and size distribution of particles. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed by D8-Advance Bruker using Cu (Kα) emission operated at 35 mA and 40 kV in the 2θ range from 10° to 80° to check the crystalline structure. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were carried out by AvaSpec-2048-USB2 across in the range from 200 to 900 nm. Fourier transform-infrared spectrum (FT-IR) recorded with the Jasco 680-plus spectrophotometer using KBr pellets. Fluorescence spectra were taken on the JASCO FP-750 fluorophotometer.

A high optical transparent polymer is needed as the LSC matrix. PMMA with high optical transparency is produced by the bulk polymerization of MMA. The thermal radical initiator was used for polymerization. The LSC was made by dispersion of N-GQDs into monomer solution, containing 0.01 wt% of quantum dots to form a homogeneous suspension. A hand-made template with dimensions of 40 mm × 40 mm × 4 mm used for the fabrication of rigid GQDs-PMMA nanocomposite.

At first, 10 ml of MMA containing 0.2 wt% of AIBN was kept stirring until a transparent solution obtained and then N-GQDs with a certain concentration was dispersed. In the next step, the solution was injected into the casting mold and kept in thermostatic water bathe in 80 °C for 15 min. The temperature was decreased to 60 °C when the mixture reached certain viscosity and kept for 12 h. Finally, the nanocomposite was cut to the size of 20 mm × 20 mm × 3 mm and polished with silica powder to reach a smooth surface for optical measurements.

The GQDs-LSC and pure PMMA plate were integrated with a perovskite solar cell prepared by the method of Saliba et al. [19] with 11% power conversion efficiency (PCE) in which the fabrication is explained in details in supporting information. The perovskite solar cell was attached from the sidelong of GQDs-LSC (20 mm × 3 mm). The rest of the cell was covered with a black absorber paper to protect from receiving light. They were irradiated with solar simulator light perpendicular to the GQDs-LSC surface for further tests.

The photocurrent-voltage measurements were performed using Biologic SP-300 potentiostat/galvanostat while LSC surface was irradiated by the solar simulator with an air mass of 1.5 global illuminations (AM 1.5 G, 100 mW/cm2).

Glucose as an inexpensive carbon source is selected as a carbon basis and TEPA is a nitrogen source for the preparation of high yield fluorescent N-GQDs. The size distribution of nano-sized quantum dots is detected by TEM and DLS analysis. Fig. 1 a demonstrates the TEM image of N-doped GQDs which proves the formation of spherical shape particles and their size distribution extracted from DLS result is in the domain of 1–18 nm and the average size of 4 nm.

The XRD pattern of the N-doped GQDs in Fig. 1b shows a broad diffraction pattern at 2θ = 24°. The diffraction peak is related to the (002) crystalline planes of graphitic carbon, suggesting that the precursors converted to graphitic structure [20,21].

FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 ) was used to characterize N-GQDs and it is compared with glucose GQDs. The result shows a wide band at 3100-3500 cm−1 with two peaks that refer to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. The peak located at 2960 cm−1 refers to the stretching vibration of C–H and suggests that some precursors have not been carbonized completely. The existence of CN stretching peak at 1610 cm−1 confirms that nitrogen atoms are satisfyingly placed in the graphitic carbon lattice. The vibrational peaks of CO in –COOH and OC–NH are located in 1705, 1490 and 1400 cm−1, respectively. The band at 1115 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of O–H [22–25]. The absence of N–H and C–N peaks in FT-IR spectrum of glucose GQDs, prepared in the same way as N-GQDs, show the successful entrance of N atoms in the graphitic structure of N-GQDs.

The optical features of N-GQDs are studied by UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra. In Fig. 3 there are three absorption peaks at 240, 290 and 320 nm and a long tail continuing to the NIR range. The strong absorption band at 240 nm drives from π-π* transitions of the graphitic sp2 domain in sp3 C matrix. This transition cannot produce a high-intensity fluorescence signal [26]. There are two shoulders at 290 and 340 nm that can be related to the n-π* transition of CO [27]. Additionally, in N-GQDs these transitions can be related to the conjugated C–N/CN [12,28]. The absorption spectrum of glucose GQDs in Fig. S1, supporting information demonstrates one absorption peak which is in the range of 300–400 nm. The main peak refers to π-π* transitions of graphitic structure. Whereas the peak shoulder arises from the surface state energy transitions and is responsible for fluorescence emission [22].

In Fig. 4 a the fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared N-GQDs in different excitation wavelengths are shown. The maximum of fluorescence emission is observed at 460 nm which is excited by 320 nm. It is obvious that the photoluminescence of obtained N-GQDs is not excitation-dependent, implying the existence of a large number of different functional groups on the surface. Moreover, incorporation of N atoms leads to the formation of new functional groups on the surface and as a result, a series of emissive traps are made [30]. The full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) is about 80 nm. A brief sight of the PL and absorption spectra reveals that the Stokes shift (Δνst) reaches as large as 100 nm and the N-GQDs show weak self-absorption.

In addition to the strong fluorescence emission, newly synthesized N-GQDs show upconversion feature. In order to study the upconversion effect, a cut-off filter of 400 nm was used to remove the second harmonic generation of monochromator. From Fig. 4b it can be noticed that the photoluminescence spectra of N-GQDs excited by wavelengths in the domain of 660–800 nm have upconverted emission located at 485 nm with a weak red shift. The maximum intensity of upconversion obtained when the sample is excited at 720 nm. Multi-photon absorption is the most probable mechanism for the upconversion emission of N-GQDs [31]. The same studies on glucose GQDs (Fig. S2, supporting information) indicate that it has no upconversion PL.

Photoluminescence excitation spectrum (PLE) in Fig. 5 a recorded to study the excitation process. At an emission wavelength of 450 nm, two excitation signals at 240 and 320 nm is obtained, but the strong photoluminescence emission detected when the sample is excited at 320 nm. The strong emission spectra, excited in the range of 290–340 nm resulted in trapping of excited-state energy with the electronic surface states [28,29]. There are two peaks with low intensity around 490 and 630 nm that seem to be at the locations of the double wavelength of the normal PLE spectra. The PLE test was repeated in the presence of a cut-off filter of 400 nm to eliminate the interference of monochromator and prove UCPL in new N-GQDs. The excitation of sample was recorded at the emission wavelength of 480 nm and the appearance of a peak at 720 nm is the evidence of upconversion effect. Fig. 5b shows the fluorescence and upconversion emission of N-GQDs at excitation wavelengths of 320 and 720 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature dependence and excitation dependence of upconversion emission
ce:section.ce:para: The PL and UCPL intensities are dependent on temperature. According to the mechanisms of PL and UCPL, two different behaviors are expected. In normal PL, an increase of temperature increases the probability of nonradiative transitions and the PL intensity decreases, while in UCPL the additional energy of a transition can be supplied by thermal energy [32]. In Fig. 6 a the effect of temperature on the integrated PL intensity of normal PL and UCPL is shown. The normal PL intensity declines as the temperature increases while it does not affect UCPL intensity, which shows differences in the mechanism of normal PL and UCPL. Moreover, the excitation power density dependence of both normal PL and UCPL is another important parameter. For normal PL, a linear relationship between the intensity of the light source and PL intensity happens, while for other excitation mechanisms like correlated two-photon process, TS-TPA or Auger, the linear relationship is not observed and it is proportional to the square or cubic power density of light source [33]. Since in the upconversion process more than one photon is involved, a different behavior than normal PL is expected. Fig. 6b plots the relationship between the integrated intensities of both normal PL and UCPL and the excitation intensity. The results show a linear dependence for normal PL while for UCPL it is fitted with the second power of excitation intensity.

The quantum yield (ϕ) defined as a simple ratio of fluorescence deactivation probability after one photon has been absorbed [34]. The photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield can be obtained by using Eq. (1):
(1)
ϕ =
ϕ R
×
(
I /
I R
)
×
(
A R
/ A
)
×
(
η /
η R
)
2
in which I is the measured integrated emission intensity, A is the optical density and η is the refractive index of the solvent. In this research, Rh.B was employed as standard luminophore with ϕR = 0.31, in which the subscript “R” refers to standard [35]. The QY of N-GQDs determined to be 65%.
The above results of downconversion and upconversion spectra implying that the as-synthesized N-GQDs is operative for the UV, visible and NIR spectrum of sunlight.

To evaluate the merits of newly synthesized upconversion N-GQDs, its performance as a luminophore in LSCs was studied. Specifically, N-GQDs were incorporated into PMMA to fabricate a GQDs-LSC model sized as 20 mm × 20 mm × 3 mm. To investigate the effect of upconversion, Incident photon-to-current efficiency (IPCE) of perovskite solar cell and emission spectra of N-GQDs in water and N-GQDs in PMMA matrix (GQDs-LSC) with an excitation wavelength of 720 nm were measured. Fig. 7 a presents that the UCPL of GQDs is totally located in the maximum efficiency of perovskite solar cells. Moreover, the results show the host material effect on emission wavelengths, in which in PMMA as a nonpolar matrix the maximum of UCPL emission at 450 nm becomes stronger [36]. As a result, the employment of N-GQDs preferably harvests the utilization of NIR photons.

Further studies were followed by assembling LSC-PV devices, in the presence and absence of N-GQDs in the PMMA matrix and the results are shown in Fig. 7b. The GQDs-LSC and Pure-PMMA plates with similar size of 20 mm × 20 mm × 3 mm, integrated with perovskite solar cells by the PCE of 11%. Key factors include open-circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current density (Jsc), fill factor (FF) and PCE, extracted from J-V curves, are provided in Table 1 . A significant increase in the values of Voc and Jsc from 0.68 V to 6.7 mA/cm2 to 0.81 V and 24.7 mA/cm2 in the presence of N-GQDs is observed. PCE which is a significant parameter to compare the efficiency of PVs and attached LSCs, extracted from J-V curves. The PCE has increased more than 4.3 folds in the existence of N-GQDs in the PMMA matrix from 2.01% for PMMA-PV to 8.77% for GQDs-LSC-PV.

In this research, a new nitrogen-doped GQD was synthesized with a facial and high-yield hydrothermal method using glucose as an inexpensive carbon source. The obtained N-GQDs exhibit strong normal PL and UCPL under UV–Vis–NIR radiation with high photoluminescence quantum yield. Doping of N atoms could greatly increase the energy levels and creating more and more electron transition pathways leading to an increase in normal PL and UCPL intensity. Excitation independent of normal PL and UCPL emission is an extremely important parameter for luminophores used in LSCs. In this case, the maximum overlap between the IPCE of perovskite solar cell and the PL of GQDs plays the leading role in LSC-PV devices. The power conversion efficiency of perovskite solar cell attached to N-GQDs-LSC increases more than 4.3 folds from pure PMMA. A brief sight of the results indicates that using LSCs with new N-GQDs as special luminophores is capable of converting the unwanted solar radiation in NIR region to the proper wavelength to produce electricity. In this case, it is reasonable to believe that this new GQDs-LSC with high PL and UCPL, provides opportunities for application in low-cost solar energy systems.

Sahar Saeidi: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Behzad Rezaei: Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Conceptualization. Neda Irannejad: Visualization, Investigation. Ali Asghar Ensafi: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry and Research Council of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) for supporting this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of UV illumination on perylene-doped luminescent solar concentrators: a cautionary tale
